entry.title: Re: Can I learn SAP Material Management from Books on my...
entry.content:
Hi ,

Yes, you can learn SAP MM from books on your own — but only to a certain extent.

1. Start with theory → Books + YouTube tutorials

Good for understanding concepts & configuration steps.

2. Get SAP system access

Use an ECC or S/4HANA practice server from any training provider (monthly subscription).

3. Practice key cycles

  • Procure-to-Pay
  • Stock postings
  • Physical inventory
  • Subcontracting
  • Consignment
  • Invoicing (MIRO)
  • Real project exposure

    Consulting skills come from:

    • Solving real business problems
    • Understanding client requirements
    • Working on SAP implementation or support tickets
    • Knowing integration with FI/SD/WM

entry.title: Re: SAP BTP-CPI-Package-Domain-Centric-Pattern (PDCP):Solving Package Sprawl at Scale-PART II
entry.content:
@vinaymittal 

Thank you for taking the time to share a detailed and thoughtful perspective. These discussions are essential in enterprise architecture, particularly because approaches that work well for isolated integrations often reveal their limitations when applied at global scale.

Your feedback appears to assess the proposal primarily from a tactical implementation perspective, focused on individual interface simplicity. The intent of the DCRP and PDCP frameworks, however, is to address enterprise platform governance, long-term sustainability, and systemic risk — a different architectural layer. Allow me to clarify that strategic intent:

1. Leadership, Discipline, and Architectural Order

The use of strategic metaphors, including “The Commander”, is not about conflict, but about discipline, accountability, and decision ownership. In mission-critical landscapes processing tens of thousands of messages daily, insufficient governance does not fail gracefully — it compounds operational risk. The framework emphasizes architectural order as a preventive control against uncontrolled landscape entropy.

2. Resilience Through Abstraction (Role of SAP API Management)

The framework does not position SAP API Management as mandatory, but as architecturally justified in scenarios aligned with SAP’s Clean Core strategy and perimeter security principles.

The gateway layer acts as a facade and abstraction boundary, enabling backend evolution, vendor changes, or migrations without impacting consumers. This is not added complexity — it is resilience by design, activated only when scale and exposure demand it.

3. Business Domains Over Vendor-Centric Structures

Vendor-based grouping is often effective at small scale, but it introduces long-term coupling.

PDCP aligns packages with business domains, treating source systems as interchangeable technical components. This mirrors organizational ownership, simplifies impact analysis, and ensures that the architectural structure survives vendor turnover — a recurring reality in enterprise landscapes.

4. Reducing the Administrative Surface Area

Secret vaults solve the storage of credentials, but not governance topology.

From an operational governance standpoint, managing fewer, tiered technical users significantly reduces permission sprawl, audit complexity, and the probability of configuration errors within SAP BTP Cockpit. The objective is not merely fewer credentials, but fewer governance failure points.

5. “Order by Design” vs. Deferred Complexity

In enterprise environments, “starting simple” often leads to unstructured growth, which later becomes costly legacy.

An upfront, domain-centric framework enables rapid onboarding and deployment (measured here at an 18.8× improvement), precisely because governance and routing concerns are already standardized and absorbed by the platform.

“Strategy without tactics is the slowest route to victory.Tactics without strategy is the noise before defeat.” — Sun Tzu

DCRP and PDCP are not about adding complexity; they are about introducing intentional structure so that scale can be achieved without chaos.

The upcoming Part III will focus on concrete implementation details and measurable operational outcomes, which should further clarify how these principles translate into practice.

Best regards,
Ricardo Viana
Enterprise Integration Architect

entry.title: How to Restrict Access to Employee Data Based on Responsibility Period? - SAP SuccessFactors
entry.content:
Time‑Restricted Access to Employee Records: Why “When” and “Where” Matter! 

Rina just became the manager of Dev, who moved to her team on April 1, 2025. On her first day, Rina tries to open Dev’s entire work history to understand his past assignments back to 2019. She’s surprised: she can only see records from April 1, 2025 onward—nothing before. Meanwhile, Amol in Payroll can see Dev’s UK records but not his earlier US ones. Why the difference? Because their access is now restricted by time of responsibility and legal entity responsibility, not just by a broad role like “Manager” or “Payroll Admin.”

This blog explains, in simple terms, how period‑restricted access works in SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central for managers, job relationship roles, and permission‑group (admin) roles—and why it’s a meaningful shift from traditional role‑based access that exposed all historical time slices.

What changed—and why it matters

Historically, role‑based permissioning in many HR systems allowed anyone with a role (e.g., Manager or HR Admin) to view an employee’s entire history of effective‑dated records (job info, personal info, compensation, etc.). That created two problems:

  1. Over‑exposure of history: New managers could see records from periods when they had no responsibility.
  2. Complex compliance scenarios: Admins responsible for specific legal entities sometimes saw records for employees outside those entities.

Period‑restricted access fixes this by overlaying time (and, for admins, legal entity responsibility) on top of role‑based permissions. Result: people see only the records that fall within their responsibility period, and admins see only records for in‑scope legal entities within the dates they were responsible.

Supported HRIS entities include effective‑dated data like Job Information, Job Relationships, Personal Information, Dependent Information, Address Information, and Compensation Information on the latest People Profile (view, edit, and History UI) and EC Quick Actions.
 

Key concepts at a glance

  • Responsibility period (time): The start and end dates during which a manager (or a job‑relationship role like matrix manager, HR manager, or custom manager) is formally associated to the employee. Access is limited to only those dates across the employee’s effective‑dated records.
  • Permission‑group admins (scope): For roles granted via permission groups, access is further limited to the legal entities selected as “responsible” for that role. Within those entities, time rules still apply—you see only the time slices that fall within your responsibility period.
  • Direct associations only: Period‑restricted access covers direct managers and supported job‑relationship types (e.g., HR manager, matrix manager, second/additional/custom manager). It doesn’t auto‑roll up to higher or lower org levels.
  • Multiple time‑based roles: If a user holds multiple time‑bounded roles (say, direct manager and matrix manager at different times), their access becomes the union of those responsibility periods.
  • Not for analytics/API/imports: The restriction applies to People Profile/Quick Actions, not to analytics reports, APIs, or import sources.

Example 1 — New manager can’t see “before my time”

Scenario

  • Employee: Susan
  • Change: International transfer from the US to the UK effective January 1, 2022
  • Managers:
    • Robert (US HR Administrator / prior manager) — responsible until December 31, 2021
    • Paula (UK HR Administrator / new manager) — responsible from January 1, 2022 onward

How the system checks access

  1. It looks at the dates each manager is associated to Susan (their responsibility periods).
  2. It applies these dates across Susan’s effective‑dated Personal Information (and other HRIS entities) to decide what each manager can view/edit/insert.

What each manager can see/do

  • April 1, 2022 – Dec 31, 9999 (current period):

    • Paula: Can access (view/edit).
    • Robert: No access (outside his responsibility period).

Dec 1, 2021 – Mar 31, 2022 (bridge window around the transfer):

  • Robert: Can view/edit and insert until Dec 31, 2021.
  • Paula: Can view/edit and insert from Jan 1, 2022.
  • This reflects the precise hand‑off date where old manager stops editing/inserting records and new manager starts. However, both can view the entire time slice as it's overlapping time of responsibility.

Jan 1, 2021 – Nov 30, 2021 (older history):

  • Robert: Can access (was responsible then).
  • Paula: No access (was not responsible yet).

Key Takeaway: 

Paula, the new manager, cannot see or edit time slices before Jan 1, 2022. Robert, the former manager, cannot see or edit anything after Dec 31, 2021. The system enforces the “only during my watch” rule automatically, time‑slicing every supported HRIS entity.
 

Example 2 — Legal‑entity–based restriction for permission‑group admins

Scenario

  • Employee: Susan
  • Legal Entities:
    • ORG1_US (before transfer)
    • ORG1_UK (after transfer)
  • Payroll Admins (granted via permission groups):
    • John → responsible for ORG1_US (until Dec 31, 2021)
    • Alex → responsible for ORG1_UK (from Jan 1, 2022)

How the system checks access

  1. It evaluates whether the employee’s legal entity on a given time slices in job histories are among the admin’s responsible legal entities configured and selected in the permission role.
  2. Then it overlays time of responsibility to determine which slices the admin can view/edit/insert for the same or other effective-dated HRIS entities.

What each admin can see/do

  • Apr 1, 2022 – Dec 31, 9999 (ORG1_UK):

    • Alex (UK admin): Access granted.
    • John (US admin): No access.

  • Dec 1, 2021 – Mar 31, 2022 (transfer window; LE changes on Jan 1, 2022):

    • Alex: Access and may create new records from Jan 1, 2022 (when Susan joins ORG1_UK).
    • John: Access and may create new records until Dec 31, 2021 (while Susan is still in ORG1_US).

    Jan 1, 2021 – Nov 30, 2021 (ORG1_US):

    • John: Access granted.
    • Alex: No access.

    Key Takeaway: 

    Permission‑group admins are not global by default. Their access is limited to the legal entities they’re responsible for and the dates of that responsibility—mirroring the employee’s effective‑dated records.
     

    How to enable and configure (quick checklist) in 2H 2025 Release

    Prerequisites (Admin Center → Company System and Logo Settings):

    • Turn on Full Profile for the latest People Profile. - This feature requires new/latest people profile enabled to work.
    • Enable Period‑Restricted Access for Managers.
    • Enable Period‑Restricted Access for Job Relationships.
    • Enable Period‑Restricted Access for Permission Groups.
      Then, in Configure Latest People Profile, enable the latest Editing and History UI for EC HRIS entities (except Compensation Information).

    Role‑based permissions (Admin Center → Manage Permission Roles):

    • For Managers: Grant to Managers, target Granted User’s Direct Reports.
    • For Job Relationships: Grant to supported relationship types (HR manager, matrix, additional/custom), target Matrix Reports as applicable.
    • For Permission Groups: Grant to Select Groups; in Manage Period‑Restricted Access, choose the Responsible Legal Entity(ies) for that role. 

    Notes & boundaries

    • If a role is not enabled for period restriction in Company Settings, users in that role are treated as administrators with unrestricted access.
    • Period‑restricted access currently does not apply to analytics, APIs, or import change sources.
    • The feature supports direct managers/relationships only—no automatic roll‑ups.
    • Overlapping records with time of responsibility of multiple managers or admins will be visible by both old and new. However, edit/insert will be restricted based on time of responsibility.

    Summary

    • Privacy by design: People only see what they’re responsible for—and only when they’re responsible.
    • Clean hand‑offs: On the effective date of a transfer or manager change, access shifts automatically—no lingering visibility into past or future slices.
    • Granular compliance: Legal‑entity scoping for admins ensures regional or entity‑specific teams don’t over‑reach.

entry.title: Re: Prepaid Scenario in SAP CC
entry.content:
Hello

If you use the allowance, the amount is directly stored in the allowance. By doing this you don't need any more to manage a prepaid account with a balance amount.  

Please see the usage definition of Allowances in Convergent Invoicing  : "Within the framework of an integrated scenario using SAP Convergent Charging and Subscription Order Management, you can process allowances in Convergent Invoicing. An allowance is a credit in the form of a quantity or an amount, or in the form of both a quantity and an amount, that is used for services that a customer purchases and uses up successively."

best regards,

Eric F.

entry.title: Custom Number Range for Business Partner while doing CVI in a ECC to S/4HANA Conversion
entry.content:
Hello All,

I am working on a CVI activity as part of an ECC to S/4HANA Private Cloud conversion project. In the current ECC system, Customers and Vendors share overlapping number ranges—for example, Customers use 100000–199999 and Vendors use 100000–200000.

The client expects the corresponding Business Partners in S/4HANA to follow new, prefixed number ranges:

  • Customer BPs: 1000100000–1000199999
  • Vendor BPs: 2000100000–2000200000

Essentially, they want to add prefixes of 1000 for customer BPs and 2000 for vendor BPs.

Based on my understanding, during CVI we can either:

  1. Use the same number for the BP as the existing Customer/Vendor, or
  2. Use an internal BP number range.

However, we cannot manipulate BP number assignment to automatically add prefixes or transform the number during CVI synchronization.

I would appreciate a peer review to confirm whether my understanding is correct.

If you want, I can also help you draft a more assertive version, a more formal version, or one that includes SAP notes and technical references.

entry.title: New course: Positioning SAP Order Management Services
entry.content:
We’re excited to introduce a brand-new learning opportunity for sales and pre-sales professionals:

Positioning SAP Order Management Services 

Why SAP Order Management Services?

In today’s digital-first economy, businesses face increasing challenges in managing their order processes. From handling multiple sales channels and meeting rising customer expectations to integrating with legacy systems and reducing errors, order management has never been more complex. SAP Order Management Services (OMS) is engineered to solve exactly these challenges.

What Will You Learn?

This focused course goes beyond product features. It guides you through:

  • The challenges faced by modern businesses in order management: Understand the pain points your customers are experiencing, such as supply chain visibility gaps, inefficiencies in order handling, and the need for real-time inventory management.
  • The end-to-end capabilities of SAP OMS: Discover how SAP OMS covers the full order process lifecycle, from order capture and validation to fulfillment, tracking, and returns, seamlessly integrated across all channels and touchpoints.
  • Business value drivers and tangible benefits: Learn how to articulate the business case, showing customers how SAP OMS improves operational efficiency, elevates the customer experience, reduces costs, boosts revenue, and supports scalability and innovation.
  • Inspiring customer success stories: See how real-world organizations have transformed their order management processes and driven measurable business outcomes with SAP OMS, stories you can share to build trust and credibility with your clients.

Who Should Enroll?

  • Sales consultants working with SAP customers or prospects
  • Pre-sales consultants looking to stand out with specialized knowledge
  • Anyone eager to understand how SAP OMS drives smarter business operations

Learn Today!

Ready to master SAP OMS positioning? Start learning now and take the first step toward equipping yourself with the knowledge and strategies you need to confidently position SAP Order Management Services.

 

We hope you enjoy this course and encourage you to share your valuable feedback with us. Thank you!

entry.title: Re: SAP BTP-CPI-Package-Domain-Centric-Pattern (PDCP):Solving Package Sprawl at Scale-PART II
entry.content:
Thank you for the response. I appreciate the clarification on strategic intent.

However, I'm still looking for concrete answers to the practical concerns raised:

Cost justification: What is the actual TCO comparison? API Management licenses, implementation hours, ongoing maintenance vs. the deployment time savings claimed?

Real-world validation: You mention 18.8× deployment improvement. Can you share:
 Actual before/after metrics from a production landscape?
 Team size and skill level required?
 Timeline for implementation?

Edge cases**: How does this handle:
- Emergency hotfixes requiring single iFlow deployment?
- Different security requirements per vendor (e.g., PCI compliance for payments)?
- Gradual migration from existing sprawl?
- Different quota/Whitelisting policy needs per API Endpoint 

The framework presents interesting concepts, but without implementation evidence and cost-benefit analysis, it's difficult to evaluate whether this is genuinely more sustainable than established patterns like logical grouping with strong naming conventions.

entry.title: Re: How to assign role to communication user in s/4hana p...
entry.content:
Good day @Yuexin_Jin 

Thank you for your question.

That error message in your XML is key. What you are experiencing is an authorisation/start permission issue for the service, not a UI5 coding issue.

The reference below provides guidance:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/2743304

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3462808

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0f69f8fb28ac4bf48d2b57b9637e81fa/a0771f6765f54e1c8193ad8582a32edb.html

From your screenshot, your arrangement has both API_BILLING_DOCUMENT_SRV (OData V2) and api_billingdocument/srvd_a2x (OData v4) but you called /sap/opu/odata/sap/API_BILLING_DOCUMENT_SRV/...  and got start authentication error. That error (see KBA 3462588) could originate from a few factors such as:

  • Your communication arrangement is inconsistent or not fully active
  • You choose wrong authentication path
  • Your communication user credentials are not being sent correctly by the destination
  • API not available in your tenant scope/release combination. Etc.

Please note that, you do not need your BTP trial account to be in the same tenant landscape as s4 cloud to make your intent work. Deploying the app inside the same system will not fix your specific error.

I recommend you:

  1. Verify SAP_COM_O120 is active, and the exact OData endpoint is used from your communication arrangement.
  2. Then verify communication user is not lock and the attached credentials are valid.
  3. Test the endpoint with basic auth in Postman first and,
  4. Ensure your BTP destination sends communication-user credentials and that they point to the same API host shown in your arrangement.

In summary, a separate BTP account is not the core issue you are facing. If the same error persists in Postman and UI5, I suggest you raise an incident with arrangement, endpoint and error.

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris
